title: Stag’s Leap 1973 achieves new record auction price
summary:
A bottle of Stags Leap 1973, the wine that won the Judgment of Paris fifty years ago and put California on the fine wine map, has achieved a new world record, selling for more than five times its low estimate.

The post Stag’s Leap 1973 achieves new record auction price appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content: A bottle of Stags Leap 1973, the wine that won the Judgment of Paris fifty years ago and put California on the fine wine map, has achieved a new world record, selling for more than five times its low estimate. A bottle of the “legendary” 1973 Cabernet Sauvignon from Stags Leap  achieved US$22,500 against an estimate of US$4,000 - 6,000. It was the centrepiece of Christie's online auction which  featured 50 lots from the winery's reserve cellar, showcasing its "pioneering legacy and its enduring influence on the world of fine wine," Christie's said. The wine was made by Warren Winiarski, and became one of America’s iconic wines after outscoring some of France’s greatest red wines at the historic blind tasting. The event, organised by Steven Spurrier and Patricia Gallagher saw nine esteemed French judges evaluate top reds and whites from France and California without knowing their origins, with California’s win sending shockwaves through the industry. Juan Muñoz-Oca, estates director at Stag's Leap Wine Cellars called the result “a powerful testament to the enduring legacy of the 1973 S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon” and the vision of Stag’s Leap’s founder, Warren Winiarski. “Fifty years after the Judgment of Paris, it's remarkable to see this historic moment and the wine at the centre of it continue to captivate collectors and reaffirm Napa Valley's place among the world's great winegrowing regions,” he said. He had previously said the sale marked some "of the most significant releases from our library cellar". Chris Munro, head of wine America at Christie’s said Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars holds "a singular place in the history of American wine" and the 1973 deserved its place “at the top table with the worlds iconic wines” during the 50th anniversary year of the Judgment of Paris.  Other lots in the auction included a Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon Salmanazar, which was paired with a private visit to Napa with winemaker Marcus Notaro, a behind‑the‑scenes estate tour, tasting, and dinner (pre-sale estimate: US$4,000–5,000) and a selection of rare vintages of Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon from 1978, 1977, 1975, and 1974. A rare vertical lot was also offered featuring 16 rare vintages, including the Judgment of Paris 50th Anniversary S.L.V. Magnum, of which only 1,973 were produced. However a rare 1974–2023 vertical, which Munro had called an extraordinary testament to five decades of winemaking excellence" and a rare opportunity "for collectors to engage directly with the legacy that helped redefine Napa Valley’s global standing", sold below the pre-sale estimate of US$4,000 - 6,000. Similarly a 55-bottle lot of mixed Stag's Leap wines from 1974-2023 with an estimate of US$10,000 - 15,000 failed to sell.

title: Should Spain’s top sparkling wines drop the name Cava?
summary:
During a discussion at London’s Taste Spain trade event on the fine wines from Spain, it was argued that the country’s most famous fizz, Cava, should change its name – at least for for its top tier cuvées. Patrick Schmitt MW reports.

The post Should Spain’s top sparkling wines drop the name Cava? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
During a discussion at London’s Taste Spain trade event on the fine wines from Spain, it was argued that the country’s most famous fizz, Cava, should change its name – at least for for its top tier cuvées. Patrick Schmitt MW reports.

The topic was fine wines from Spain, and the guests were some of the most knowledgeable on the subject, with technical director Carlos López de la Calle from Bodega Artadi – representing the producer’s view; Camino Restaurants founder Richard Bigg – representing the Spanish food and wine scene in London – and Sarah Jane Evans, a Master of Wine, journalist, author, judge, and wine commentator with a deep and long-standing love of all things liquid and Spanish. Having covered various issues, such as the secondary market for fine Spanish wine, age-worthy whites from Rioja, and the role of La Place de Bordeaux as a potential way to raise awareness for high-end brands from Iberia, the discussion turned to Spain’s fine fizz, primarily Cava – the traditional method Spanish sparkling wine that mainly hails from the Penedès region of Catalonia, but can also be made in Rioja and Requena, among a few other parts of the country. And it was at this point that some of the strongest views were shared – as you can hear by listening to a recording of the discussion here and below.

Cava and Corpinnat

For Bigg, collectible status for Cava could be garnered “by some of the very best producers", naming in particular Recaredo and Gramona – although both have left the DO Cava regulatory board to help create Corpinnat, a stricter collective mark for high-end sparkling wines from Penedès. Not only do such Spanish sparkling wine brands have a strong reputation, but they are producing age-worthy fizz, often released with 10 years of cellaring, while bringing the market a point of difference from Champagne by using native Spanish grape Xarel-lo. However, for Carlos López de la Calle, if Spain is to build a fine wine reputation for fizz, it needs to find a new name, and one that’s tied to a specific place. “I think in order to make Spanish sparkling wine great again, we cannot call it Cava – Cava has suffered so much damage in terms of producing volume that we need to forget about that name,” he began.

A new name needed

As for Corpinnat – which was formed in 2015 by six initial sparkling wine producers, and comprises 11 today – he added, “I know and love all of them, and I'm 100% behind their idea, but my only concern with Corpinnat is that no-one puts Corpinnat in Google and if they do, it won’t appear as an area.”
For Evans, who initially chose to remain silent on the topic, then added to the debate, saying, “When I was finishing my book last year on the wines of Central and Southern Spain – including – Catalonia, I really had to think about Cava, and my conclusion was that Cava should be used for everything aged for less than 18 months,” she said. Continuing she commented, “But actually all these wonderful and lovely and individual diverse wines that are Cava over 18 months should be called something else, and we should recognise that these are from Catalonia – and that there are different ones that come from Rioja or Requena.” She concluded, referring to Corpinnat, “I can see why people are leaving Cava, because they have tried everything to make Cava a valid name, but it needs another name.”

Top places for Spanish sparkling

López de la Calle agreed, commenting, “And I think we need to be more specific in terms of the sparkling; we need to define the areas that are specific for sparkling production.” Among these, he said the most obvious would be Penedès, while another could be Galicia, while for him, there is great potential for northern Spain’s Chacolí (or Txakoli) – which he said benefits from naturally low alcohol levels and high acidity. In each case, he said, “These are locations that are unique for their sparkling wines.” As for Cava, he concluded, “It is too generic”. Certainly the Cava DO has make considerable efforts to raise its reputation for quality, and, indeed, levels of environmental sustainability too – with all Guarda Superior Cavas released from 2025 certified as 100% organic. Speaking to db last year about this particular development, Javier Pagés, president of DO Cava explained that the premium nature of the Spanish fizz, along with its accessibility, were key elements to its success. “The future of Cava lies in continuing to focus on quality and the value of the Cava brand," he said.

Read more

Why Cava is 'undergoing an exciting renaissance' Cava DO now 100% organic for Cava de Guarda Superior Premium organic Cava rises in popularity in the US Spain’s most overlooked wines, according to the experts

title: Six new Master Sommeliers announced
summary:
Six new master sommeliers have been crowned by the Court of Master Sommeliers (CMS), including first recipients from Mauritius, Bosnia and the Czech Republic following a rigorous two days of theory and tasting exams at Vienna’s Grand Hotel last month.

The post Six new Master Sommeliers announced appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Six new master sommeliers have been crowned by the Court of Master Sommeliers (CMS), including first recipients from Mauritius, Bosnia and the Czech Republic following a rigorous two days of theory and tasting exams at Vienna’s Grand Hotel last month. This takes the total number of MS’s worldwide to 299. “So many congratulations to everyone who’s passed,” said CEO Ronan Sayburn, who became a Master Sommelier in 2005. “We’re also delighted to welcome people from Bosnia and the Czech Republic for the first time into our community of Master Sommeliers.” He added: “Our candidates at the Vienna exam have already passed the Practical section and may have been doing these exams – sometimes resetting to zero parts (all parts must be passed within three years) – more than once. From these six candidates, some have been pursuing our course for eight to twelve years. We are delighted with their success this time.” The six new MS’s include Reza Nahaboo, the first person to pass from Mauritius, who is currently working at the Lausanne Hotel School, the first Czech MS Amar Boudjaoui, and the first Bosnian to secure MS status – Suvad Zlatic – who currently lives in Austria and works as a wine consultant.  London-based ex-67 Pall Mall sommelier Michele Fazari has also passed. Originally from France, Fazari works for Jackson Family Wines today. Wine importer Adam Michoki has also secured MS status. Born in Poland and now Leeds-based, Michoki currently runs an import company specialising in wines from Central Europe. Finally, Michal Drozdowski, head sommelier at Bottiglieria 1881 restaurant in Poland, has also secured the prestigious title. .

Toasting to half a century

Next year will mark the 50th anniversary of the Court of Master Sommeliers, originally founded by Bryan Julyan MS in 1977, and now present in 33 cities across 23 countries.  Hong Kong is now joining the CMS map, which will hold the final, Level 4, Master Sommelier Diploma exam for the first time later this year and will alternate with Australia every year from now on.  Sayburn said: “We run the earlier levels all over Asia and Australasia – the demand is high. But now it’s time for the Master Exam to be held in Asia too, and I’m thrilled that we’ll see 45 candidates set out to be the best they can be on the day – they’re working extremely hard and I’d expect to see some passes.”

 Turning over a new page

The CMS team has also just completed its 6th revision of its world-recognised course textbook, which has been relaunched as ‘Sommelier’ (previously Sales and Service for the Wine Professional) with the Academie du Vin Library. “This is our first update in seven years, so it’s been a pretty big mission to bring all text and maps up to date for the modern era of beverage service,” added Sayburn. “’Sommelier’ is also more than that – it’s a fact-based book for any wine student. A must-read for all aspiring and current beverage professionals and a great reference for enthusiasts. In its five previous editions since 1988, it’s been the handbook for all aspiring sommeliers to learn from.”

title: Château Galoupet puts sustainability at the heart of rosé
summary:
Château Galoupet’s 2025 vintage shows how detailed parcel work and regenerative farming are shaping a more precise expression of Provence rosé. Backed by LVMH, the estate is also investing in water management, soil health and lower-impact packaging as it prepares for a hotter future.

The post Château Galoupet puts sustainability at the heart of rosé appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Château Galoupet’s 2025 vintage shows how detailed parcel work and regenerative farming are shaping a more precise expression of Provence rosé. Backed by LVMH, the estate is also investing in water management, soil health and lower-impact packaging as it prepares for a hotter future. Ever since Mathieu Meyer became technical director of Provence's Château Galoupet in 2019, the estate has focused on cutting-edge sustainability and innovation, alongside an in-depth study of how individual varieties, terroirs and parcels contribute to the final rosé blend. The estate comprises 65ha of vineyards, surrounded by 77ha of forest, on the south-facing schistous slopes of the Massif des Maures, in La Londe, only a few hundred metres from the sea. Certified organic since 2023 and regenerative organic since 2025, Meyer believes that what makes Galoupet so unique is "the context of the forest, the diversity of plots and terroirs", integrating every element to create a harmonious ecosystem. Different cover crops are planted depending on the macro-terroir. Instead of grazing sheep among the vines, they are kept strictly on fallow land, cover crops are ploughed directly into the soil to build organic matter. The estate has noticed a large increase in insects and a more complete chain of predators, giving it a stable ecosystem and reducing the need for pest control. The mild climate, with the nearby sea acting as a giant air-conditioning unit, prevents frost. Meyer has also integrated a hydrology system to protect the vineyard from drought. It does not rain often but when it does it is heavy and the water normally rushes directly into the sea without infiltrating the soil. “We have created trenches along contour lines to capture the water, plus restoration of a historic reservoir constructed in the middle of the vines”, allowing the estate to irrigate in an emergency.

Tasting the 2025 vintage

At a lunch and tasting on 5 May 2026, we had the chance to taste the component parts of the 2025 vintage alongside the final blend and two older vintages, 2024 and 2023. This enabled us to discuss the character of each variety and vintage. Meyer commented: “As long as we are small we can continue to innovate”, vinifying parcel by parcel to create the optimum blend. Because 2025 yielded bigger berries, achieving phenolic maturity was a challenge. Consequently, Meyer used less Cinsault and more Tibouren. Winemaking here also involves taking the lees from every tank and stabulating them together for two weeks to build complexity. The resulting 2025 Cru Classé was pretty and crystalline, with elegant floral notes and intense fresh fruit, while the G, made entirely from estate fruit this year without buying in grapes, showed lighter, fresher zestiness. The 2024 was more powerful, schistous and structured, while the copper-pink 2023 was silky, calm and seamlessly intense, barely showing its age. Working to create a style that reflects the vintage rather than imposing a style on the wine, and still maintaining fresh elegance in increasingly hot summers, is no mean feat.

Planning beyond the next vintage

But there is more to Galoupet than simply producing good wine every vintage. Meyer is working hard on planning for the future. Looking beyond sustainable viticulture, Meyer and director Nadine Fau are aiming to create a fully integrated vision for the property. Their lightweight, recycled brown glass bottle was a radical and controversial choice in a market dominated by non-recycled clear glass. As supermarkets and distributors cut back on wines with capsules, as previously reported by the drinks business, Galoupet has adopted a 100% biodegradable wax capsule based on 85% pine resin and 15% paraffin. Five years later, the distinctive packaging remains a strong part of the estate’s identity.

Investing for the long term

Backed by LVMH, Meyer recognises the privilege of allowing short-term profitability to take a back seat. Instead, the focus is squarely on the long game: investing in soil health today to secure climate resilience for the future.

title: Are fine-wine collectors one and the same around the world?
summary:
Areni Global research suggests that fine wine collecting habits are almost identical across the globe – whether the oenologists hail from Shanghai, Paris or New York. But how true is this on the ground? Joyce Yip finds out.

The post Are fine-wine collectors one and the same around the world?  appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Areni Global research suggests that fine wine collecting habits are almost identical across the globe – whether the oenologists hail from Shanghai, Paris or New York. But how true is this on the ground? Joyce Yip finds out. As db reported, a February 2026 study by wine-dedicated research organisation Areni Global noted that the wine collection journey and habits for people under 40 years old are almost identical for respondents from London, Paris, New York, Shanghai, Hong Kong and Singapore.  But on-the-ground observations may suggest otherwise.  “Buyers”, according to the study, are defined as those who purchase for an occasion and desire the bottle quickly; the “collector”, on the other hand, acquires to fulfil a complex, ongoing need and enjoys the thrill of the pursuit. Per the study, “fine wine” is categorised as bottles priced at £50, €65, US$75, HK$550, SG$120 or RMB$880. The research revealed that the respondents across all markets learn about fine wines through friends and social networks, rather than family, and subsequently become collectors shall they enjoy financial prowess, transparent channels of access and a like-minded community. This transition between buyer to collector usually happens between the ages of 26 and 40.

Complexity over opacity

While Pauline Vicard – co-founder and executive director at Areni Global – compares fine wine collectors’ pursuit for the next bottle to the thrill of winning a video game, she emphasises that only “complexity” is sought-after rather than “opacity”.  “The digital tools, platforms and apps have not only made the chase easier, but somehow more fun,” she tells the drinks business. “Modernisation of auction houses since the pandemic has helped a new generation of collectors enter the fine wine market, thanks to their tools that allow everyone to bet at any time,” she says. Although the fine wine market’s allocation system will be here to stay due to the scarcity of sought-after bottles, Vicard adds that traditional merchants’ demand for huge minimum spends, long loyalty and purchase histories “are less and less accepted” amongst young buyers. 

Gender, education, status

Vicard says the only differences between drinkers in the East and West are that the former engages in wine education earlier on, whereas those in London, New York and Paris often form their connections through social or familial relationships.  She attributes this difference to wine’s niched access in the East, leaving education as the key channel to meet “people who share the same interest; whereas if you’re in London or New York, one of your bosses will like wine and will take you somewhere with wine involved.” In Singapore, for instance, the WSET programme is backed by the government’s SkillsFuture initiative – a nod to the region’s rapidly growing wine education industry – with tastings, masterclasses and wine bars flourishing across the island. Secondly, Vicard sees more women buying expensive wines in Asia, likely because of financial independence and the drink’s comparatively later popularity in the region, after it was no longer seen as a men-exclusive status symbol.  “Wine has been a cultural and status object in the West for a very long time, in that only men had the financial independence and power to buy wine; but when it arrived in Asia, women reached equality in terms of wealth, and wine became a gender-neutral collectible,” she says. 

Strength in numbers

Corinne Mui – Hong Kong-based wine educator, writer and judge for a number of wine shows around the world – partially agrees with Vicard’s findings. She challenges the study’s definition of HK$550 as fine wine, explaining that while that price point may deliver a “tasty selection”, she’s dubious about its ageing potential, which, ultimately, determines whether a bottle is worth collecting.  “Fine wines must have ageing potential: Hong Kong is so strapped for space, so I doubt anyone will sacrifice room for a HK$550 experiment without resale value,” she points out, adding that while some collectors prefer transparency on their chase, most crave the exclusivity, convoluted journey and status quo places like wine clubs offer.  “I get asked a lot about new club memberships: I can fairly say fine wine drinkers and collectors love being a part of a club the most,” she adds.  Regarding the higher population of women purchasing expensive wines, Mui wonders how much of the surveyed percentage are wives and girlfriends shopping for their partners. She also notes that outside of Hong Kong, most cities in Asia have a lower population of financially independent women compared to the West.  Titled 'The New Fine Wine Consumer: How people under 40 find their way into fine wine', Areni Global’s study was done in partnership with Berry Bros and Rudd, 67 Pall Mall, Vinophiles Society and The Young Members Circle of the International Wine and Food Society. Data was collected through qualitative interviews, focus groups and questionnaires from a total of 308 respondents and 209 current fine-wine buyers.

title: Carlsberg emerges as World Cup winner despite smaller than expected beer boost
summary:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup delivered a smaller uplift to global beer volumes than Morgan Stanley had expected, after several major brewing markets exited the tournament earlier than forecast. Carlsberg nevertheless appears to have gained more than its rivals from the teams that progressed furthest.

The post Carlsberg emerges as World Cup winner despite smaller than expected beer boost appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup delivered a smaller uplift to global beer volumes than Morgan Stanley had expected, after several major brewing markets exited the tournament earlier than forecast. Carlsberg nevertheless appears to have gained more than its rivals from the teams that progressed furthest. Morgan Stanley has cut its estimate for the World Cup’s contribution to global beer volumes after early eliminations for several important markets reduced the expected sales benefit. The bank now predicts that the tournament will add 17 basis points to global beer volumes for the full year, down from its previous estimate of 24 basis points. Brazil, Germany and Colombia were among the markets whose earlier than expected exits limited the duration of heightened consumer interest and drinking occasions.

Carlsberg gains from European progress

Carlsberg was described as the clearest positive surprise among the major brewers, benefiting from strong tournament runs by the UK, France, Switzerland and Norway. The Danish brewer has a significant presence across those markets, leaving it better placed to capture continued demand as national teams progressed into the later stages. Morgan Stanley had previously argued that the largest gains would be concentrated in markets whose teams reached the quarter-finals and beyond, when national interest, viewing figures and group drinking occasions tend to peak. The effect of each additional match can therefore prove more important than the overall size of the tournament.

Brazil exit limits AB InBev uplift

AB InBev is expected to receive a smaller boost than initially forecast in Latin America following Brazil’s early elimination. The brewer nevertheless retains a prominent role as the tournament’s official beer sponsor, giving it exposure across host cities, broadcast coverage and fan events beyond the performance of individual national teams. Morgan Stanley sees the overall effects for Heineken and Royal Unibrew as broadly unchanged, with gains in some markets offset by weaker results elsewhere.

Inventory correction risk

The bank warned that brewers may now need to adjust third-quarter shipments to bring stock levels into line with actual demand. The greatest risk of inventory corrections was identified in Brazil, Germany, Portugal and the Netherlands, where suppliers may have prepared for stronger or more prolonged tournament-related consumption. Such adjustments could weigh on shipments even where beer sales to consumers remained above normal seasonal levels during the competition.

Early trade uplift

As previously reported by the drinks business, the World Cup generated higher footfall and drinks sales for many bars, retailers and hospitality venues during its opening stages. NIQ research conducted before the tournament found that 22% of alcohol consumers globally planned to watch games in pubs, bars or restaurants. The figure rose to 26% among consumers aged between 18 and 34. Almost two thirds of respondents said drinks promotions would influence their choice of venue, with 59% expecting to spend more on drinks during matches than they would on an ordinary visit. Early sales figures suggested the tournament delivered a meaningful benefit. Beer sales across host markets rose 15.4% year-on-year, with Massachusetts recording a 30% increase and venues in Boston reporting exceptionally strong demand from visiting Scotland supporters. UK beer sales also received a major lift during England’s match against Panama, when more than eight million pints were reportedly sold.

Gains varied by market

Bars close to stadiums and major transport hubs recorded some of the strongest results. New York venues Avenida and The Rutherford said they were ordering around twice their usual volume of beer after investing more than US$75,000 in screens and audio-visual equipment. Other operators reported growing demand for long drinks, spritzes and alcohol-free beer as customers settled in for several hours of football. Retailers also saw increased interest in drinks associated with competing nations. Searches on Total Wine & More’s website rose 52.9% for Colombian aguardiente during the group stage, with Fernet searches climbing 35.2% around Argentina’s matches and aquavit searches increasing 33.6% during Norway’s campaign.

title: Trump hits Canada with 50% tariffs over US alcohol ban
summary:
US President Donald Trump has imposed 50% tariffs on selected Canadian imports, accusing the country's provinces of discriminating against American alcoholic beverages. The White House said the move is intended to pressure Canada into lifting restrictions that have slashed US alcohol exports by more than 80%.

The post Trump hits Canada with 50% tariffs over US alcohol ban appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
US President Donald Trump has imposed 50% tariffs on selected Canadian imports, accusing the country's provinces of discriminating against American alcoholic beverages. The White House said the move is intended to pressure Canada into lifting restrictions that have slashed US alcohol exports by more than 80%. President Donald Trump has signed a proclamation imposing additional 50% tariffs on certain Canadian imports, escalating the trade dispute between the two countries over Canada's restrictions on US alcoholic beverages. The duties, which target around US$20 billion worth of Canadian goods, will take effect from 19 August. The White House said the measures were introduced under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, which allows the President to impose duties on imports from countries found to be discriminating against US commerce.

White House targets provincial alcohol bans

In the proclamation, the administration argues that Canadian provinces unfairly singled out American alcohol after removing US products from government-controlled liquor stores and wholesale systems from March 2025. Ontario's Liquor Control Board (LCBO) and Quebec's Société des alcools du Québec (SAQ) were cited among the organisations that stopped purchasing and distributing US wines, beers and spirits. Only Alberta and Saskatchewan subsequently lifted their restrictions in June 2025. According to The White House, Canadian imports of US alcoholic beverages fell by around 81% between March 2025 and February 2026 compared with the same period a year earlier, dropping from approximately US$718 million to US$137m. The administration also argued that Canada continued to import alcohol from other countries, claiming shipments from Chile, Japan, Argentina, Ireland, New Zealand and Australia all increased over the same period. Imports from European Union member states also rose by more than US$100m, according to the proclamation.

Tariffs intended to pressure Canada

Trump said the new duties would offset what he described as Canada's "unreasonable, unequal and discriminatory" treatment of US alcohol producers. The proclamation argues that Canadian restrictions have harmed American wineries, breweries and distilleries by suppressing exports, reducing manufacturing output and weakening investment in rural communities. The White House said imposing additional tariffs could encourage Canada to remove the provincial restrictions while strengthening opportunities for domestic producers within the US market. The new measures apply in addition to most existing duties, although certain products already covered by separate trade measures are exempt.

Dispute follows proposed CANADA Act

The latest escalation comes days after legislation was introduced in Congress calling for a formal investigation into Canada's treatment of US alcoholic beverages. As previously reported by the drinks business, Republican congresswoman Claudia Tenney proposed the Combating Attacks on our National Alcoholic Drinks by Allies (CANADA) Act, which would require the US Trade Representative to launch a Section 301 investigation into Canadian provincial restrictions. Tenney argued that Canadian liquor boards had unfairly targeted American producers over a wider trade dispute unrelated to the drinks sector. The proposed legislation received backing from the Wine Institute, WineAmerica and the American Craft Spirits Association.

Industry caught in wider trade war

Canada's restrictions were introduced in response to tariffs imposed by the United States during the wider trade dispute that began last year. Since then, nearly all Canadian provinces removed US alcoholic beverages from government-controlled retail outlets and distribution networks, prompting domestic producers and exporters from other countries to fill the resulting gap. The measures have had a profound impact on American producers, for whom Canada had been the largest export market for US spirits and one of the country's most valuable destinations for wine and beer.

title: Marston’s to ‘substantially increase the pace of investment’ in 2027
summary:
Marston’s vows to convert around 100 venues in its estate next year following a “strong performance” from its sports-led pubs concept. Jessica Mason reports.

The post Marston’s to ‘substantially increase the pace of investment’ in 2027 appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Marston’s vows to convert around 100 venues in its estate next year following a “strong performance” from its sports-led pubs concept. Jessica Mason reports. In a recent trading update, Marston’s revealed that it recently sold 2 million pints during England fixtures in the World Cup, highlighting the importance of sport and community-focused pubs. The group also noted that its new pub formats all continue to deliver excellent like-for-like sales growth.

The standout performer

Grandstand pubs, the group’s sports-led concept, has been the standout performer, generating year-to-date like-for-like sales growth of around 30% in the 36 conversions completed to date. Marston’s has also described its recent success as “strong trading during the World Cup with England match days delivering like-for-like sales growth of 22%, and sales up circa 170% year-on-year across Grandstand pubs”. Grandstand pubs have been positioned to create an immersive, stadium-like feel and feature large screens and advanced sound systems along with tiered seating, pub games and match-day food and drink promotions. The group, which operates 1,300 pubs across the UK, has seen “improved margins” despite year-to-date like-for-like sales being 1.6% lower than last year. Last year, Marston’s celebrated its second consecutive year of strong profit growth, attributing the rise, to its "new formats, digital innovation and cost efficiencies” all delivering revenue uplift. This year, the board has said that it has shown “confidence in delivering full-year market expectations” and expects to achieve the EBITDA margin expansion target "significantly ahead of schedule". According to the group’s board, in 2027 "Marston’s will substantially increase the pace of investment, delivering circa 100 conversions focused on the Grandstand format”.

'Investing in growth remains the priority'

The group has also outlined that “investing in growth remains the priority” and also highlighted that “shareholder returns remain an important component of the group's capital allocation framework”. Data gathered by the Pubs Code Adjudicator (PCA) has annually highlighted tenant sentiment towards, and their relationships with tied pub companies and Marston’s was shown to have a high overall satisfaction level for many of its pub tenants. Marstom’s CEO Justin Platt said “Our pubs have delivered a strong start to the summer, with an excellent World Cup once again underlining the enduring role of the community pub as the place the nation comes together to cheer the moments that matter. Our new Grandstand pubs have been leading the way and continue to perform ahead of expectations, while our accelerated investment programme is driving further trading momentum and enhancing guest experiences across our estate.” Platt added: "Supported by a clear strategy, disciplined cost control and continued investment in our new formats, we are well positioned for the summer trading period ahead. Given this, as well as our progress on leverage reduction, the group is well-placed to recommence shareholder returns in FY2027.”

title: Three Acre Brewery pursues micro-pub and taproom plans
summary:
East Sussex-based Three Acre Brewery has plans to turn two sites, one in Heathfield and one in Uckfield, into taprooms and micro-pubs. 

The post Three Acre Brewery pursues micro-pub and taproom plans appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
East Sussex-based Three Acre Brewery has plans to turn two sites, one in Heathfield and one in Uckfield, into taprooms and micro-pubs. 

Cask ale experts

The award-winning Sussex brewery, which was founded in 2019 and is well-known for its cask ales, has plans for the High Street venue in Heathfield to be named The Storehouse. The site will include an upgraded interior, a small service area and utilise space outside on Cherwell Road for bistro tables. The Storehouse is anticipated to open later this year, subject to council approval. Three Acre Brewery is also currently in the process of opening a separate micro-pub in an old railway signal box in Uckfield. While planning permission has been secured for this site and it is now legally a pub with its premises licence approved and building control plans already approved, the historic site is still being repaired by Uckfield Town Council and is delayed due to local authority timelines. As such, the site in Heathfield is due to open possibly before the Uckfield site this year. A planning statement submitted with the latest application, in Heathfield, said: "The change of use will provide employment for people in the local area, specifically younger people who may have less work experience. It will also allow the premises to generate an income with reinvestment of the profits into the premises for continued maintenance and preservation following a period of neglect."

Local support

The plans for each of the independent brewery-owned Sussex-based venues have received significant backing from local residents, with many letters of support submitted to the local councils boosting the applications. The openings have been welcomed by crowdfunders who initially backed plans for the signal box. The brewery has assured that their free beer tokens will also be redeemable at the Heathfield venue as well.

Community-focused

Three Acre Brewery head brewer Jamie Newton said: “We’ve had our roots in Heathfield since the very beginning. Our plan is to make the Storehouse a hub for music, quizzes, talks, exhibitions and more and we are really excited about the opportunity and what it can offer to local people.” During the pandemic, Three Acre Brewery built its reputation among local residents after it delivered beer to hundreds of houses in and around the East Sussex area. The brewery’s horsebox bar can also be found every year at Le Marche, the village’s Anglo-French market that takes place each August bank holiday.

title: The winery betting on the ‘next chapter’ for cool-climate Australian wine
summary:
Having made its name on the cool slopes of Adelaide Hills, Bird in Hand is expanding the operation to Tasmania. Founder Andrew Nugent explains how it meets the market’s need for authenticity and could prove “the next chapter” for key varieties.

The post The winery betting on the ‘next chapter’ for cool-climate Australian wine appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Having made its name on the cool slopes of Adelaide Hills, Bird in Hand is expanding the operation to Tasmania. Founder Andrew Nugent explains how it meets the market’s need for authenticity and could prove “the next chapter” for key varieties. Since 1997, Bird in Hand has made the case for specificity in Australian wine. While many were still drinking Shiraz and Chardonnay from the massive farms of Riverland, Andrew and Susie Nugent were making the case for the cool climate and diverse terroirs of Adelaide Hills. At their winery, aided by a hardworking team, they formed an operation that has always been led by its land. Whether in the terroir-focused wines of the Tribute range or a concerted effort to make viticulture sustainable, respecting the slopes of Adelaide Hills is central. Even the hospitality and tourism arm, with its award-winning garden and LVN restaurant, seems rooted in the area. Bird in Hand’s expansion to Tasmania is therefore a pretty notable shift. Suddenly, the family operation spans around 1,000 kilometres. Yet for Andrew Nugent, it is an evolution rather than a disjuncture. The new project is staying true to first principles, even if separated by hundreds of miles and choppy waters. Moreover, it is helping Bird in Hand lean into its USP in a challenging global market.

Why Tasmania?

“For Bird in Hand, expanding into Tasmania is about pushing quality even further,” Nugent explains, “producing the best product we can to craft wines that compete at the top echelon of global wine.” In that pursuit, the experience of cool-climate winemaking in Adelaide Hills can be transferred directly to Tasmania, home to Australia’s southernmost vineyards. In fact, more than terroirs, it is a philosophy that unites winemaking in the two regions. He comments: “Both are relatively young Australian regions, so both give winemakers freedom to explore rather than being bound by tradition.” They offer different opportunities for Bird in Hand, however. Adelaide Hills is hugely geographically diverse: vineyards between 300 and 600 metres in elevation, a range of aspects, significant variety in microclimates and layers of ancient soils. You can broadly term it a cool climate, but speaking of just one climatic definition cannot convey the region. The story in Tasmania is somewhat different. Instead of all these different influences, there is one dominating factor: the waters surrounding the island. Bird in Hand has found itself a northeast-facing site on ancient, alluvial soils, just west of the Tamar River. It is distinctly maritime, sitting close to the Bass Strait and with the cool influence of the Southern Ocean. “That gives Tasmania as a whole a longer, slower, cooler ripening season than almost anywhere else in Australia,” Nugent says, “which is what preserves the natural acidity, purity and varietal intensity in the wines.” With awards and critical attention settling on Tasmania, particularly for its sparkling wine and lighter styles, some are now suggesting that it is Australia’s best region for Chardonnay. On that, Nugent is sanguine, but evidently excited. “I'd put it this way, Tasmania is producing some of the most exciting, world-class Chardonnay in the country right now, alongside Pinot Noir and sparkling,” he says. “I wouldn't necessarily crown it ‘the best’ over other cool sites, because there's excellent Chardonnay coming out of the Adelaide Hills, Yarra Valley and Margaret River,” he reasons, “but Tasmania has a genuine claim to leading the next chapter. ”It's a style that draws real comparisons to cool-climate Burgundy and Chablis more than anything traditionally Australian.”

Meeting demand

As cool-climate specialists, the winemaking appeal is evident. That is only half the equation, however: commerciality is a key consideration. The market is tough. Australian exports fell by 8% in value in 2025 as key markets like the UK, the US and China cut their drinking. A number of factors, among them pressures on the cost of living, fewer wine-led occasions and a wellness-driven move away from alcohol, have combined to create a tough market. Bird in Hand’s response is to look to where growth is possible. Indeed, those parts of the market play straight into its hand. Take the UK, consistently Australia’s largest export market by volume. Overall off-trade sales in the UK are falling, as is the volume that Australia exports there. Yet the picture is not universal, suggesting that it may be more of a realignment than a long-term decline. Nugent explains: “UK exports valued above $7.50/litre are actually up around 15%, spread across Shiraz, Cabernet, Chardonnay, and Sauvignon Blanc. Cheap, commodity wine is under real pressure whereas quality wine with a genuine story and provenance behind it is holding up, and in some cases growing. That's exactly the space Bird in Hand is built for.” Moreover, being so invested in terroir and Australian potential, Bird in Hand is banking on Australia’s identity as much as any price point. In its sparkling production, for instance, the winery runs the gamut from vibrant and fruit driven wines made with the tank method to a “celebratory, but quietly serious” Tasmanian traditional method fizz. To win over drinkers, Nugent sells them on their own terms, rather than against benchmarks. “I think the trick has been not trying to be a cheaper Champagne or a more serious Prosecco, but to make something that's unmistakably a product of Australia,” he says. “It’s about giving drinkers a distinct, high-quality alternative with its own sense of place.” Even aside from the implications for sparkling, Nugent believes that the new Tasmanian project hits demand for authenticity and precision: “There is a global surge in demand for premium, cool-climate wine, and Tasmania is one of the few places in the world positioned to answer that demand with real purity, acidity and elegance.”

Getting the word out

There is palpable excitement around Bird in Hand’s work in Tasmania, marking its first project outside of its own back yard. Yet anyone worried about world domination should rest easy – there is no sign of limitless expansion. “First and foremost, the focus is on producing exceptional wines, an integral part of Bird in Hand’s DNA,” Nugent says. If there is no quality basis backing up a project, Bird in Hand will not move on it. Therefore, instead of rampant expansion in winemaking, the Nugents are making sure Bird in Hand gets the recognition it deserves. Certainly that is true in hospitality, which often goes hand in hand with winemaking. Bird in Hand, like many family-owned properties, has embraced hospitality as a homespun supplement to winemaking. What marks it out, however, is the scale of the operation. There is still a local connection. At the Woodside Estate in Adelaide Hills, guests can taste at the Cellar Door or explore the award-winning gardens. There is also a fine dining restaurant, LVN, that draws on the winery’s Australian bases, as well as luxury guest quarters for its diners. With the Tasmanian operation underway, Bird in Hand has further expanded its Australian hospitality. This includes a bespoke events space and farm cottage, overlooking its 10 hectare leased vineyard in Rowella, Tamar Valley, as well as the prestigious new Bird in Hand restaurant in the Royal Tasmanian Botanical Gardens, complete with its own tasting room Yet, fittingly for a winery that sells both a £16 best-selling sparkling and super-premium Chardonnay, its local markets are the beginning, not the limit, of the hospitality operation. It is now truly international. Both the venues – like New York’s The Carlyle or London’s Scott’s and Harry’s Bar – and collaboration partners, such as Chatsworth House in Derbyshire, Alexa Chung, Victoria Beckham and Brooke Shields, speak to Bird in Hand’s high standards and impressive connections. As the next step, the winery is taking over the Dante Mayfair Terrace at Claridge’s in London this summer. “This collaboration really grew quite organically from longstanding relationships and a shared focus on quality rather than being a one-off commercial partnership,” explains Nugent. Having listed the rare and premium Tribute Series since 2019, Claridge’s has a long partnership with Bird in Hand. Even if the takeover is temporary, it builds on enduring respect. You can see that in the range of wines on show: perennial bestsellers like the Pinot Rosé but also rarer gems like Tasmanian Riesling and Reserve Pinot Noir. Much like the move to Tasmania, it is not change for change’s sake, but an alignment of sensibilities. These are evolutions that are not altering the fundamentals, but are expanding horizons. “Dante is consistently regarded as one of the best bars in the world, and that level of global recognition and craft is exactly the standard Bird in Hand strives for,” Nugent summarises. “It’s not just about visibility, it’s about being in environments that reflect the same pursuit of excellence.”

title: Stag’s Leap 1973 achieves new record auction price
summary:
A bottle of Stags Leap 1973, the wine that won the Judgment of Paris fifty years ago and put California on the fine wine map, has achieved a new world record, selling for more than five times its low estimate.

The post Stag’s Leap 1973 achieves new record auction price appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content: A bottle of Stags Leap 1973, the wine that won the Judgment of Paris fifty years ago and put California on the fine wine map, has achieved a new world record, selling for more than five times its low estimate. A bottle of the “legendary” 1973 Cabernet Sauvignon from Stags Leap  achieved US$22,500 against an estimate of US$4,000 - 6,000. It was the centrepiece of Christie's online auction which  featured 50 lots from the winery's reserve cellar, showcasing its "pioneering legacy and its enduring influence on the world of fine wine," Christie's said. The wine was made by Warren Winiarski, and became one of America’s iconic wines after outscoring some of France’s greatest red wines at the historic blind tasting. The event, organised by Steven Spurrier and Patricia Gallagher saw nine esteemed French judges evaluate top reds and whites from France and California without knowing their origins, with California’s win sending shockwaves through the industry. Juan Muñoz-Oca, estates director at Stag's Leap Wine Cellars called the result “a powerful testament to the enduring legacy of the 1973 S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon” and the vision of Stag’s Leap’s founder, Warren Winiarski. “Fifty years after the Judgment of Paris, it's remarkable to see this historic moment and the wine at the centre of it continue to captivate collectors and reaffirm Napa Valley's place among the world's great winegrowing regions,” he said. He had previously said the sale marked some "of the most significant releases from our library cellar". Chris Munro, head of wine America at Christie’s said Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars holds "a singular place in the history of American wine" and the 1973 deserved its place “at the top table with the worlds iconic wines” during the 50th anniversary year of the Judgment of Paris.  Other lots in the auction included a Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon Salmanazar, which was paired with a private visit to Napa with winemaker Marcus Notaro, a behind‑the‑scenes estate tour, tasting, and dinner (pre-sale estimate: US$4,000–5,000) and a selection of rare vintages of Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon from 1978, 1977, 1975, and 1974. A rare vertical lot was also offered featuring 16 rare vintages, including the Judgment of Paris 50th Anniversary S.L.V. Magnum, of which only 1,973 were produced. However a rare 1974–2023 vertical, which Munro had called an extraordinary testament to five decades of winemaking excellence" and a rare opportunity "for collectors to engage directly with the legacy that helped redefine Napa Valley’s global standing", sold below the pre-sale estimate of US$4,000 - 6,000. Similarly a 55-bottle lot of mixed Stag's Leap wines from 1974-2023 with an estimate of US$10,000 - 15,000 failed to sell.

title: Should Spain’s top sparkling wines drop the name Cava?
summary:
During a discussion at London’s Taste Spain trade event on the fine wines from Spain, it was argued that the country’s most famous fizz, Cava, should change its name – at least for for its top tier cuvées. Patrick Schmitt MW reports.

The post Should Spain’s top sparkling wines drop the name Cava? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
During a discussion at London’s Taste Spain trade event on the fine wines from Spain, it was argued that the country’s most famous fizz, Cava, should change its name – at least for for its top tier cuvées. Patrick Schmitt MW reports.

The topic was fine wines from Spain, and the guests were some of the most knowledgeable on the subject, with technical director Carlos López de la Calle from Bodega Artadi – representing the producer’s view; Camino Restaurants founder Richard Bigg – representing the Spanish food and wine scene in London – and Sarah Jane Evans, a Master of Wine, journalist, author, judge, and wine commentator with a deep and long-standing love of all things liquid and Spanish. Having covered various issues, such as the secondary market for fine Spanish wine, age-worthy whites from Rioja, and the role of La Place de Bordeaux as a potential way to raise awareness for high-end brands from Iberia, the discussion turned to Spain’s fine fizz, primarily Cava – the traditional method Spanish sparkling wine that mainly hails from the Penedès region of Catalonia, but can also be made in Rioja and Requena, among a few other parts of the country. And it was at this point that some of the strongest views were shared – as you can hear by listening to a recording of the discussion here and below.

Cava and Corpinnat

For Bigg, collectible status for Cava could be garnered “by some of the very best producers", naming in particular Recaredo and Gramona – although both have left the DO Cava regulatory board to help create Corpinnat, a stricter collective mark for high-end sparkling wines from Penedès. Not only do such Spanish sparkling wine brands have a strong reputation, but they are producing age-worthy fizz, often released with 10 years of cellaring, while bringing the market a point of difference from Champagne by using native Spanish grape Xarel-lo. However, for Carlos López de la Calle, if Spain is to build a fine wine reputation for fizz, it needs to find a new name, and one that’s tied to a specific place. “I think in order to make Spanish sparkling wine great again, we cannot call it Cava – Cava has suffered so much damage in terms of producing volume that we need to forget about that name,” he began.

A new name needed

As for Corpinnat – which was formed in 2015 by six initial sparkling wine producers, and comprises 11 today – he added, “I know and love all of them, and I'm 100% behind their idea, but my only concern with Corpinnat is that no-one puts Corpinnat in Google and if they do, it won’t appear as an area.”
For Evans, who initially chose to remain silent on the topic, then added to the debate, saying, “When I was finishing my book last year on the wines of Central and Southern Spain – including – Catalonia, I really had to think about Cava, and my conclusion was that Cava should be used for everything aged for less than 18 months,” she said. Continuing she commented, “But actually all these wonderful and lovely and individual diverse wines that are Cava over 18 months should be called something else, and we should recognise that these are from Catalonia – and that there are different ones that come from Rioja or Requena.” She concluded, referring to Corpinnat, “I can see why people are leaving Cava, because they have tried everything to make Cava a valid name, but it needs another name.”

Top places for Spanish sparkling

López de la Calle agreed, commenting, “And I think we need to be more specific in terms of the sparkling; we need to define the areas that are specific for sparkling production.” Among these, he said the most obvious would be Penedès, while another could be Galicia, while for him, there is great potential for northern Spain’s Chacolí (or Txakoli) – which he said benefits from naturally low alcohol levels and high acidity. In each case, he said, “These are locations that are unique for their sparkling wines.” As for Cava, he concluded, “It is too generic”. Certainly the Cava DO has make considerable efforts to raise its reputation for quality, and, indeed, levels of environmental sustainability too – with all Guarda Superior Cavas released from 2025 certified as 100% organic. Speaking to db last year about this particular development, Javier Pagés, president of DO Cava explained that the premium nature of the Spanish fizz, along with its accessibility, were key elements to its success. “The future of Cava lies in continuing to focus on quality and the value of the Cava brand," he said.

Read more

Why Cava is 'undergoing an exciting renaissance' Cava DO now 100% organic for Cava de Guarda Superior Premium organic Cava rises in popularity in the US Spain’s most overlooked wines, according to the experts

title: Six new Master Sommeliers announced
summary:
Six new master sommeliers have been crowned by the Court of Master Sommeliers (CMS), including first recipients from Mauritius, Bosnia and the Czech Republic following a rigorous two days of theory and tasting exams at Vienna’s Grand Hotel last month.

The post Six new Master Sommeliers announced appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Six new master sommeliers have been crowned by the Court of Master Sommeliers (CMS), including first recipients from Mauritius, Bosnia and the Czech Republic following a rigorous two days of theory and tasting exams at Vienna’s Grand Hotel last month. This takes the total number of MS’s worldwide to 299. “So many congratulations to everyone who’s passed,” said CEO Ronan Sayburn, who became a Master Sommelier in 2005. “We’re also delighted to welcome people from Bosnia and the Czech Republic for the first time into our community of Master Sommeliers.” He added: “Our candidates at the Vienna exam have already passed the Practical section and may have been doing these exams – sometimes resetting to zero parts (all parts must be passed within three years) – more than once. From these six candidates, some have been pursuing our course for eight to twelve years. We are delighted with their success this time.” The six new MS’s include Reza Nahaboo, the first person to pass from Mauritius, who is currently working at the Lausanne Hotel School, the first Czech MS Amar Boudjaoui, and the first Bosnian to secure MS status – Suvad Zlatic – who currently lives in Austria and works as a wine consultant.  London-based ex-67 Pall Mall sommelier Michele Fazari has also passed. Originally from Italy, Fazari works for Jackson Family Wines today. Wine importer Adam Michoki has also secured MS status. Born in Poland and now Leeds-based, Michoki currently runs an import company specialising in wines from Central Europe. Finally, Michal Drozdowski, head sommelier at Bottiglieria 1881 restaurant in Poland, has also secured the prestigious title. .

Toasting to half a century

Next year will mark the 50th anniversary of the Court of Master Sommeliers, originally founded by Bryan Julyan MS in 1977, and now present in 33 cities across 23 countries.  Hong Kong is now joining the CMS map, which will hold the final, Level 4, Master Sommelier Diploma exam for the first time later this year and will alternate with Australia every year from now on.  Sayburn said: “We run the earlier levels all over Asia and Australasia – the demand is high. But now it’s time for the Master Exam to be held in Asia too, and I’m thrilled that we’ll see 45 candidates set out to be the best they can be on the day – they’re working extremely hard and I’d expect to see some passes.”

 Turning over a new page

The CMS team has also just completed its 6th revision of its world-recognised course textbook, which has been relaunched as ‘Sommelier’ (previously Sales and Service for the Wine Professional) with the Academie du Vin Library. “This is our first update in seven years, so it’s been a pretty big mission to bring all text and maps up to date for the modern era of beverage service,” added Sayburn. “’Sommelier’ is also more than that – it’s a fact-based book for any wine student. A must-read for all aspiring and current beverage professionals and a great reference for enthusiasts. In its five previous editions since 1988, it’s been the handbook for all aspiring sommeliers to learn from.”

title: Château Galoupet puts sustainability at the heart of rosé
summary:
Château Galoupet’s 2025 vintage shows how detailed parcel work and regenerative farming are shaping a more precise expression of Provence rosé. Backed by LVMH, the estate is also investing in water management, soil health and lower-impact packaging as it prepares for a hotter future.

The post Château Galoupet puts sustainability at the heart of rosé appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Château Galoupet’s 2025 vintage shows how detailed parcel work and regenerative farming are shaping a more precise expression of Provence rosé. Backed by LVMH, the estate is also investing in water management, soil health and lower-impact packaging as it prepares for a hotter future. Ever since Mathieu Meyer became technical director of Provence's Château Galoupet in 2019, the estate has focused on cutting-edge sustainability and innovation, alongside an in-depth study of how individual varieties, terroirs and parcels contribute to the final rosé blend. The estate comprises 65ha of vineyards, surrounded by 77ha of forest, on the south-facing schistous slopes of the Massif des Maures, in La Londe, only a few hundred metres from the sea. Certified organic since 2023 and regenerative organic since 2025, Meyer believes that what makes Galoupet so unique is "the context of the forest, the diversity of plots and terroirs", integrating every element to create a harmonious ecosystem. Different cover crops are planted depending on the macro-terroir. Instead of grazing sheep among the vines, they are kept strictly on fallow land, cover crops are ploughed directly into the soil to build organic matter. The estate has noticed a large increase in insects and a more complete chain of predators, giving it a stable ecosystem and reducing the need for pest control. The mild climate, with the nearby sea acting as a giant air-conditioning unit, prevents frost. Meyer has also integrated a hydrology system to protect the vineyard from drought. It does not rain often but when it does it is heavy and the water normally rushes directly into the sea without infiltrating the soil. “We have created trenches along contour lines to capture the water, plus restoration of a historic reservoir constructed in the middle of the vines”, allowing the estate to irrigate in an emergency.

Tasting the 2025 vintage

At a lunch and tasting on 5 May 2026, we had the chance to taste the component parts of the 2025 vintage alongside the final blend and two older vintages, 2024 and 2023. This enabled us to discuss the character of each variety and vintage. Meyer commented: “As long as we are small we can continue to innovate”, vinifying parcel by parcel to create the optimum blend. Because 2025 yielded bigger berries, achieving phenolic maturity was a challenge. Consequently, Meyer used less Cinsault and more Tibouren. Winemaking here also involves taking the lees from every tank and stabulating them together for two weeks to build complexity. The resulting 2025 Cru Classé was pretty and crystalline, with elegant floral notes and intense fresh fruit, while the G, made entirely from estate fruit this year without buying in grapes, showed lighter, fresher zestiness. The 2024 was more powerful, schistous and structured, while the copper-pink 2023 was silky, calm and seamlessly intense, barely showing its age. Working to create a style that reflects the vintage rather than imposing a style on the wine, and still maintaining fresh elegance in increasingly hot summers, is no mean feat.

Planning beyond the next vintage

But there is more to Galoupet than simply producing good wine every vintage. Meyer is working hard on planning for the future. Looking beyond sustainable viticulture, Meyer and director Nadine Fau are aiming to create a fully integrated vision for the property. Their lightweight, recycled brown glass bottle was a radical and controversial choice in a market dominated by non-recycled clear glass. As supermarkets and distributors cut back on wines with capsules, as previously reported by the drinks business, Galoupet has adopted a 100% biodegradable wax capsule based on 85% pine resin and 15% paraffin. Five years later, the distinctive packaging remains a strong part of the estate’s identity.

Investing for the long term

Backed by LVMH, Meyer recognises the privilege of allowing short-term profitability to take a back seat. Instead, the focus is squarely on the long game: investing in soil health today to secure climate resilience for the future.

title: Are fine-wine collectors one and the same around the world?
summary:
Areni Global research suggests that fine wine collecting habits are almost identical across the globe – whether the oenologists hail from Shanghai, Paris or New York. But how true is this on the ground? Joyce Yip finds out.

The post Are fine-wine collectors one and the same around the world?  appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Areni Global research suggests that fine wine collecting habits are almost identical across the globe – whether the oenologists hail from Shanghai, Paris or New York. But how true is this on the ground? Joyce Yip finds out. As db reported, a February 2026 study by wine-dedicated research organisation Areni Global noted that the wine collection journey and habits for people under 40 years old are almost identical for respondents from London, Paris, New York, Shanghai, Hong Kong and Singapore.  But on-the-ground observations may suggest otherwise.  “Buyers”, according to the study, are defined as those who purchase for an occasion and desire the bottle quickly; the “collector”, on the other hand, acquires to fulfil a complex, ongoing need and enjoys the thrill of the pursuit. Per the study, “fine wine” is categorised as bottles priced at £50, €65, US$75, HK$550, SG$120 or RMB$880. The research revealed that the respondents across all markets learn about fine wines through friends and social networks, rather than family, and subsequently become collectors shall they enjoy financial prowess, transparent channels of access and a like-minded community. This transition between buyer to collector usually happens between the ages of 26 and 40.

Complexity over opacity

While Pauline Vicard – co-founder and executive director at Areni Global – compares fine wine collectors’ pursuit for the next bottle to the thrill of winning a video game, she emphasises that only “complexity” is sought-after rather than “opacity”.  “The digital tools, platforms and apps have not only made the chase easier, but somehow more fun,” she tells the drinks business. “Modernisation of auction houses since the pandemic has helped a new generation of collectors enter the fine wine market, thanks to their tools that allow everyone to bet at any time,” she says. Although the fine wine market’s allocation system will be here to stay due to the scarcity of sought-after bottles, Vicard adds that traditional merchants’ demand for huge minimum spends, long loyalty and purchase histories “are less and less accepted” amongst young buyers. 

Gender, education, status

Vicard says the only differences between drinkers in the East and West are that the former engages in wine education earlier on, whereas those in London, New York and Paris often form their connections through social or familial relationships.  She attributes this difference to wine’s niched access in the East, leaving education as the key channel to meet “people who share the same interest; whereas if you’re in London or New York, one of your bosses will like wine and will take you somewhere with wine involved.” In Singapore, for instance, the WSET programme is backed by the government’s SkillsFuture initiative – a nod to the region’s rapidly growing wine education industry – with tastings, masterclasses and wine bars flourishing across the island. Secondly, Vicard sees more women buying expensive wines in Asia, likely because of financial independence and the drink’s comparatively later popularity in the region, after it was no longer seen as a men-exclusive status symbol.  “Wine has been a cultural and status object in the West for a very long time, in that only men had the financial independence and power to buy wine; but when it arrived in Asia, women reached equality in terms of wealth, and wine became a gender-neutral collectible,” she says. 

Strength in numbers

Corinne Mui – Hong Kong-based wine educator, writer and judge for a number of wine shows around the world – partially agrees with Vicard’s findings. She challenges the study’s definition of HK$550 as fine wine, explaining that while that price point may deliver a “tasty selection”, she’s dubious about its ageing potential, which, ultimately, determines whether a bottle is worth collecting.  “Fine wines must have ageing potential: Hong Kong is so strapped for space, so I doubt anyone will sacrifice room for a HK$550 experiment without resale value,” she points out, adding that while some collectors prefer transparency on their chase, most crave the exclusivity, convoluted journey and status quo places like wine clubs offer.  “I get asked a lot about new club memberships: I can fairly say fine wine drinkers and collectors love being a part of a club the most,” she adds.  Regarding the higher population of women purchasing expensive wines, Mui wonders how much of the surveyed percentage are wives and girlfriends shopping for their partners. She also notes that outside of Hong Kong, most cities in Asia have a lower population of financially independent women compared to the West.  Titled 'The New Fine Wine Consumer: How people under 40 find their way into fine wine', Areni Global’s study was done in partnership with Berry Bros and Rudd, 67 Pall Mall, Vinophiles Society and The Young Members Circle of the International Wine and Food Society. Data was collected through qualitative interviews, focus groups and questionnaires from a total of 308 respondents and 209 current fine-wine buyers.

title: Carlsberg emerges as World Cup winner despite smaller than expected beer boost
summary:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup delivered a smaller uplift to global beer volumes than Morgan Stanley had expected, after several major brewing markets exited the tournament earlier than forecast. Carlsberg nevertheless appears to have gained more than its rivals from the teams that progressed furthest.

The post Carlsberg emerges as World Cup winner despite smaller than expected beer boost appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup delivered a smaller uplift to global beer volumes than Morgan Stanley had expected, after several major brewing markets exited the tournament earlier than forecast. Carlsberg nevertheless appears to have gained more than its rivals from the teams that progressed furthest. Morgan Stanley has cut its estimate for the World Cup’s contribution to global beer volumes after early eliminations for several important markets reduced the expected sales benefit. The bank now predicts that the tournament will add 17 basis points to global beer volumes for the full year, down from its previous estimate of 24 basis points. Brazil, Germany and Colombia were among the markets whose earlier than expected exits limited the duration of heightened consumer interest and drinking occasions.

Carlsberg gains from European progress

Carlsberg was described as the clearest positive surprise among the major brewers, benefiting from strong tournament runs by the UK, France, Switzerland and Norway. The Danish brewer has a significant presence across those markets, leaving it better placed to capture continued demand as national teams progressed into the later stages. Morgan Stanley had previously argued that the largest gains would be concentrated in markets whose teams reached the quarter-finals and beyond, when national interest, viewing figures and group drinking occasions tend to peak. The effect of each additional match can therefore prove more important than the overall size of the tournament.

Brazil exit limits AB InBev uplift

AB InBev is expected to receive a smaller boost than initially forecast in Latin America following Brazil’s early elimination. The brewer nevertheless retains a prominent role as the tournament’s official beer sponsor, giving it exposure across host cities, broadcast coverage and fan events beyond the performance of individual national teams. Morgan Stanley sees the overall effects for Heineken and Royal Unibrew as broadly unchanged, with gains in some markets offset by weaker results elsewhere.

Inventory correction risk

The bank warned that brewers may now need to adjust third-quarter shipments to bring stock levels into line with actual demand. The greatest risk of inventory corrections was identified in Brazil, Germany, Portugal and the Netherlands, where suppliers may have prepared for stronger or more prolonged tournament-related consumption. Such adjustments could weigh on shipments even where beer sales to consumers remained above normal seasonal levels during the competition.

Early trade uplift

As previously reported by the drinks business, the World Cup generated higher footfall and drinks sales for many bars, retailers and hospitality venues during its opening stages. NIQ research conducted before the tournament found that 22% of alcohol consumers globally planned to watch games in pubs, bars or restaurants. The figure rose to 26% among consumers aged between 18 and 34. Almost two thirds of respondents said drinks promotions would influence their choice of venue, with 59% expecting to spend more on drinks during matches than they would on an ordinary visit. Early sales figures suggested the tournament delivered a meaningful benefit. Beer sales across host markets rose 15.4% year-on-year, with Massachusetts recording a 30% increase and venues in Boston reporting exceptionally strong demand from visiting Scotland supporters. UK beer sales also received a major lift during England’s match against Panama, when more than eight million pints were reportedly sold.

Gains varied by market

Bars close to stadiums and major transport hubs recorded some of the strongest results. New York venues Avenida and The Rutherford said they were ordering around twice their usual volume of beer after investing more than US$75,000 in screens and audio-visual equipment. Other operators reported growing demand for long drinks, spritzes and alcohol-free beer as customers settled in for several hours of football. Retailers also saw increased interest in drinks associated with competing nations. Searches on Total Wine & More’s website rose 52.9% for Colombian aguardiente during the group stage, with Fernet searches climbing 35.2% around Argentina’s matches and aquavit searches increasing 33.6% during Norway’s campaign.

title: Trump hits Canada with 50% tariffs over US alcohol ban
summary:
US President Donald Trump has imposed 50% tariffs on selected Canadian imports, accusing the country's provinces of discriminating against American alcoholic beverages. The White House said the move is intended to pressure Canada into lifting restrictions that have slashed US alcohol exports by more than 80%.

The post Trump hits Canada with 50% tariffs over US alcohol ban appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
US President Donald Trump has imposed 50% tariffs on selected Canadian imports, accusing the country's provinces of discriminating against American alcoholic beverages. The White House said the move is intended to pressure Canada into lifting restrictions that have slashed US alcohol exports by more than 80%. President Donald Trump has signed a proclamation imposing additional 50% tariffs on certain Canadian imports, escalating the trade dispute between the two countries over Canada's restrictions on US alcoholic beverages. The duties, which target around US$20 billion worth of Canadian goods, will take effect from 19 August. The White House said the measures were introduced under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, which allows the President to impose duties on imports from countries found to be discriminating against US commerce.

White House targets provincial alcohol bans

In the proclamation, the administration argues that Canadian provinces unfairly singled out American alcohol after removing US products from government-controlled liquor stores and wholesale systems from March 2025. Ontario's Liquor Control Board (LCBO) and Quebec's Société des alcools du Québec (SAQ) were cited among the organisations that stopped purchasing and distributing US wines, beers and spirits. Only Alberta and Saskatchewan subsequently lifted their restrictions in June 2025. According to The White House, Canadian imports of US alcoholic beverages fell by around 81% between March 2025 and February 2026 compared with the same period a year earlier, dropping from approximately US$718 million to US$137m. The administration also argued that Canada continued to import alcohol from other countries, claiming shipments from Chile, Japan, Argentina, Ireland, New Zealand and Australia all increased over the same period. Imports from European Union member states also rose by more than US$100m, according to the proclamation.

Tariffs intended to pressure Canada

Trump said the new duties would offset what he described as Canada's "unreasonable, unequal and discriminatory" treatment of US alcohol producers. The proclamation argues that Canadian restrictions have harmed American wineries, breweries and distilleries by suppressing exports, reducing manufacturing output and weakening investment in rural communities. The White House said imposing additional tariffs could encourage Canada to remove the provincial restrictions while strengthening opportunities for domestic producers within the US market. The new measures apply in addition to most existing duties, although certain products already covered by separate trade measures are exempt.

Dispute follows proposed CANADA Act

The latest escalation comes days after legislation was introduced in Congress calling for a formal investigation into Canada's treatment of US alcoholic beverages. As previously reported by the drinks business, Republican congresswoman Claudia Tenney proposed the Combating Attacks on our National Alcoholic Drinks by Allies (CANADA) Act, which would require the US Trade Representative to launch a Section 301 investigation into Canadian provincial restrictions. Tenney argued that Canadian liquor boards had unfairly targeted American producers over a wider trade dispute unrelated to the drinks sector. The proposed legislation received backing from the Wine Institute, WineAmerica and the American Craft Spirits Association.

Industry caught in wider trade war

Canada's restrictions were introduced in response to tariffs imposed by the United States during the wider trade dispute that began last year. Since then, nearly all Canadian provinces removed US alcoholic beverages from government-controlled retail outlets and distribution networks, prompting domestic producers and exporters from other countries to fill the resulting gap. The measures have had a profound impact on American producers, for whom Canada had been the largest export market for US spirits and one of the country's most valuable destinations for wine and beer.

title: Marston’s to ‘substantially increase the pace of investment’ in 2027
summary:
Marston’s vows to convert around 100 venues in its estate next year following a “strong performance” from its sports-led pubs concept. Jessica Mason reports.

The post Marston’s to ‘substantially increase the pace of investment’ in 2027 appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Marston’s vows to convert around 100 venues in its estate next year following a “strong performance” from its sports-led pubs concept. Jessica Mason reports. In a recent trading update, Marston’s revealed that it recently sold 2 million pints during England fixtures in the World Cup, highlighting the importance of sport and community-focused pubs. The group also noted that its new pub formats all continue to deliver excellent like-for-like sales growth.

The standout performer

Grandstand pubs, the group’s sports-led concept, has been the standout performer, generating year-to-date like-for-like sales growth of around 30% in the 36 conversions completed to date. Marston’s has also described its recent success as “strong trading during the World Cup with England match days delivering like-for-like sales growth of 22%, and sales up circa 170% year-on-year across Grandstand pubs”. Grandstand pubs have been positioned to create an immersive, stadium-like feel and feature large screens and advanced sound systems along with tiered seating, pub games and match-day food and drink promotions. The group, which operates 1,300 pubs across the UK, has seen “improved margins” despite year-to-date like-for-like sales being 1.6% lower than last year.
